title: El Paso Crime Lab
text: Loading…

title: El Paso Crime Lab
text:
About Us
The El Paso Crime Lab is a research-focused organization studying crime and public safety in the El Paso region. It produces and shares intelligence products grounded in rigorous methodology to support evidence-based policy and decision-making.
We engage in a range of applied research activities, including:

Survey development and analysis
Designing, administering, and analyzing surveys to better understand community experiences, perceptions of safety, and underreported dimensions of crime.
Program and policy evaluation
Assessing the impact of interventions, enforcement strategies, and public policies using quasi-experimental and statistical methods.
Crime data analysis and visualization
Identifying trends and patterns in crime and public safety outcomes using administrative and observational data.
Report and policy brief production
Translating research findings into clear, actionable summaries for stakeholders and community partners.
Data integration and management
Combining data from multiple sources to support comprehensive analyses of crime and related social outcomes.

title: El Paso Crime Lab
text:
Open Access Mission
A central goal of the El Paso Crime Lab is transparency. By making data and research publicly accessible, the Lab promotes open dialogue and supports evidence-based approaches to criminal justice policy. This work helps bridge the gap between academic research and real-world decision-making in the El Paso community.

title: El Paso Crime Lab
text:

For questions, data requests, or collaboration opportunities, please contact:
Matthew J. Durán
email: mduran29@asu.edu
website: https://mattduran12.github.io

title: Research on Drunk Driving
text:
Overview:
The Drunk Driving Surveillance Dashboard is a publicly available tool designed to monitor alcohol-related crashes in El Paso. It includes over 30 indicators covering crash trends, geographic patterns, and driver and vehicle characteristics. The dashboard is updated monthly, enabling near real-time monitoring of impaired driving in the city.

 View Dashboard

title: Research on Drunk Driving
text:
Working Papers & Reports


Does Public Shaming Deter Drunk Driving?



Summary:
This study evaluates the impact of a social media campaign in El Paso that publicly shared booking photos of individuals arrested for driving while intoxicated (DWI). Using time-series analysis, this study attempts to isolate the campaign’s effect on alcohol-related crashes.
The findings highlight the potential and implications of social-media deterrence strategies. Recommendations for improving the strategy are provided.

 Read Paper

title: Research on Drunk Driving
text:
In the Media



Opinion Article:
This article explores why arrest data can be misleading when trying to understand drunk driving trends, and why researchers often rely on alcohol-involved crash rates instead. This article also includes a graph comparing the rate of alcohol-involved crashes in El Paso to other Texas cities.

 Read Article 

Latest News on DWI in El Paso:
This page tracks the latest developments on drunk driving in El Paso. It brings together recent reporting on crashes, arrests, enforcement activity, and policy changes so you can quickly see what’s happening across the city.

 View Latest News

This dashboard displays data on alcohol-related vehicle crashes in El Paso, Texas and is updated every month.

title: Drunk Driving Surveillance Dashboard
text:
Row

Last Update: April 2026
The Drunk Driving Surveillance Dashboard provides monthly, city-level data on alcohol-related motor vehicle crashes in El Paso, Texas. It is designed as a public-facing resource for journalists, policymakers, and researchers seeking timely and transparent information on impaired driving trends.
Crash data is downloaded from the Texas Department of Transportation (TXDOT) website using the Crash Records Information System (CRIS) Query Tool. All crash data in this dashboard represent those reported by law enforcement, were classified as alcohol-related, and occurred in the city of El Paso.
Definition of Alcohol-Related Crash
A crash is defined as alcohol-related if involved drivers or individuals were reported as “Under the Influence of Alcohol”, “Had Been Drinking”, or were reported to have a blood alcohol content (BAC) greater than 0.
Definition of Alcohol-Related Injury/Fatality
Injuries include people who were classified as having a suspected serious injury, suspected minor injury, or possible injury. Fatalities are defined as any motor vehicle traffic injury crash that results in the death of one or more persons within 30 days of the incident.
Definition of Intoxicated Driver
Driver and vehicle characteristics in these graphs represent intoxicated drivers. An intoxicated driver is defined as a person who’s BAC test result returned a value of 0.08g/dL or greater.
Acronyms
BAC = Blood alcohol content; YTD = Year to date; TXDOT = Texas Department of Transportation; CRIS = Crash Records Information System


Key Notes for Reporting

Crashes, injuries, and fatalities in the dashboard represent those that were alcohol-related

Trends are based on official crash records from TXDOT
Monthly updates may revise prior counts as data are finalized

Using Figures and Charts
All figures in this dashboard are interactive and downloadable.

To expand a figure, click the “Expand” button located at the bottom-right corner of each chart. Charts can easily be resized by adjusting the size of your internet browsing window.

To download a figure, hover over the chart and click the camera icon to save it as an image

Charts may be used in media coverage with attribution. Data Source: TXDOT CRIS Query Tool | Figure created by El Paso Crime Lab

How to Cite This Dashboard
When referencing this dashboard, please use the following citation:
El Paso Crime Lab. Drunk Driving Surveillance Dashboard: El Paso, Texas (2026). [www.epcrimelab.org/crash_dashboard.html]
Suggested Language

“Crash data on alcohol-related crashes in El Paso show…”
“Local data on alcohol-related traffic fatalities indicate…”

Contact
For questions or data clarification, please contact:
mduran29@asu.edu


Calculation of Moving Average
The line graphs on the Trends page show 12-month moving averages for crashes, fatalities, and injuries between 2016 and Present. This technique results in a smooth line, allowing the viewer to see if a trend is increasing or decreasing.
Holiday Analyses
Holidays statistics were calculated by aligning crash data to each holiday. For fixed-date holidays (e.g., New Year’s Day, Independence Day, Halloween, Christmas, and St. Patrick’s Day), each crash was assigned an event time based on the number of days before or after the calendar date of the holiday within each year. For shifting holidays (e.g., Super Bowl and Memorial Day), the holiday date was first computed for each year and then used as the reference point for event-time calculation.
In all cases, crashes were restricted to a ±7-day window around the holiday and then aggregated across years (2016–2025), excluding 2020 to avoid COVID-19-related disruptions. Average daily crash counts were then computed for each relative day to identify typical patterns before and after each holiday.
Missing Data
A small amount of data for Drivers characteristics (e.g., gender, race/ethnicity, vehicle color) is missing. Observations with missing or unknown values in these fields were excluded.
Limitations
Not all alcohol-involved crashes are identified in official records. In many cases, alcohol use may go undetected or unreported, particularly when testing is not conducted or impairment is not clearly observed. As a result, these data likely underestimate the true number of alcohol-related crashes—a gap often referred to as the “dark figure” of impaired driving.
Despite this limitation, crash data remain one of the most reliable and policy-relevant indicators of drunk driving. Unlike arrest data, which are influenced by enforcement practices, crash records capture real-world harm—injuries and fatalities—providing a consistent basis for tracking trends over time and understanding the public safety impact of impaired driving.
